candidateFor: Travelers to Seoul seeking a brief, same-day traditional Korean medicine session to address neck, shoulder, or back muscle tension
Anyone with general muscle soreness or fatigue from desk work or long-haul travel who wants a non-pharmaceutical approach
Those curious about traditional Korean medicine (Hanbang) who want a low-commitment entry point before exploring acupuncture or herbal treatments
Patients managing stress-related muscular tightness looking for a complementary therapy session during their Korea stay
Individuals who want to combine cupping with acupuncture in a single Korean medicine clinic visit

whatToExpect.detail: A licensed Korean medicine doctor reviews your areas of concern, checks the treatment area for any contraindications, and decides between dry cupping (suction only) or wet cupping (a tiny superficial scratch draws a small amount of blood). Disclose any medications, bleeding disorders, or skin conditions in the area.

Cups are positioned on the back, shoulders, or targeted muscles. Air is removed to create a vacuum that gently draws the skin upward. For dry cupping, cups remain in place for several minutes; sliding cups may move along the muscles. For wet cupping, a small scratch is made first, the area is cleaned afterward, and aftercare advice is given before you leave.

whatToExpect.duration: No downtime; marks fade in 3–10 days
whatToExpect.detail: Round purple, red, or pink marks appear where the cups sat — these are expected and not injuries. Normal activity resumes immediately. For the first 24 hours: keep the area clean, stay hydrated, and avoid heat, alcohol, and strenuous exercise. Gentle movement and warm compresses help the marks fade faster.

whyKorea: Korean medicine doctors who perform cupping complete a six-year professional program and pass a national examination issued by the Ministry of Health and Welfare — cupping is practiced within a fully regulated healthcare system

Cupping (buhang) is an everyday clinical treatment integrated into traditional Korean medicine (Hanbang), available at licensed clinics across Seoul and the Gangnam district with no special advance scheduling required

A standalone dry cupping session costs approximately $3–$15; combined with acupuncture, a visit commonly runs $15–$40 — among the most affordable professional bodywork pricing available internationally
No hospital stay, no anesthesia, and no recovery period needed — the entire appointment fits into a normal Seoul day, and many clinics accept same-day or walk-in bookings

faqs.question: What is the difference between dry and wet cupping?
faqs.answer: Dry cupping uses suction alone on intact skin to increase local circulation and ease muscle tension. Wet cupping (a form of Korean buhang) adds a tiny superficial scratch so the suction draws out a small amount of blood. A licensed Korean medicine doctor assesses which approach is appropriate for your condition and health history.

faqs.question: Does cupping therapy hurt?
faqs.answer: Most people feel a tight pulling or pressure sensation rather than pain during dry cupping. Wet cupping involves a very brief, small scratch. Mild tenderness in the treated area for a day or two afterward is normal. The round marks that appear are expected and not bruising injuries — they typically begin fading within 3–7 days.

faqs.question: How long do the cupping marks last?
faqs.answer: The round marks typically begin fading within 3–7 days and usually clear by about 10 days. Stronger suction can leave darker marks that occasionally take a little longer. Avoiding heat, alcohol, and strenuous exercise for the first 24 hours and keeping the area moisturized helps them fade faster.

faqs.question: Do I need to book in advance, or can I walk in?
faqs.answer: Many Seoul Korean medicine clinics accept same-day or walk-in appointments, making cupping easy to add to a Seoul itinerary. Confirm with your specific clinic, particularly during busy tourist seasons, as popular Gangnam-area clinics can fill up. A quick call or online booking the morning of is usually sufficient.

faqs.question: Can I have cupping if I take blood thinners or bruise easily?
faqs.answer: Discuss this with the Korean medicine doctor before treatment. Dry cupping is approached cautiously for people who bruise very easily or take anticoagulant medication. Wet cupping is generally not recommended for those with bleeding disorders or active blood-thinning therapy. Always disclose your full medication list and medical history at the start of the consultation.
